message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: Frozen StudyDefinition (and peer records) accept a mutable set for frozenset-typed fields, breaking immutability/hashability
DIMENSION: public API & adapter-Protocol stability
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: src/assay_engine/contracts/study.py:27-51; src/assay_engine/methodology/firewalls.py:90-96 
DESCRIPTION: StudyDefinition is @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) and declares modes: frozenset[StudyMode], implying a hashable, immutable value object (it is used as a registry-keyed, pre-registration-anchoring record). But __post_init__ never coerces/validates modes to a frozenset, so passing a plain set (which membership-checks identically, so all internal `StudyMode.X in modes` logic and required_phases(modes) work) is silently accepted. PoC verified: StudyDefinition(..., modes={StudyMode.DISCOVERY}) yields .modes of type set, and hash(definition) then raises `TypeError: unhashable type: 'set'` — a 'frozen' record that is neither hashable nor immutable, and is also mutable in place. The codebase's own test_contract.py:60 constructs StudyDefinition with claims_source=object() relying on direct construction, and the discovery() factory is the only path that coerces to frozenset; the raw constructor (the public API surface a clone uses) does not. DiscoverConfirmSplit (frozenset fields) has the same un-coerced-constructor shape, though from_partition coerces.
SUGGESTED FIX: In StudyDefinition.__post_init__ coerce/validate: `object.__setattr__(self, 'modes', frozenset(self.modes))` (and ideally check each element is a StudyMode). Apply the same frozenset coercion in DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__ so the documented frozen+hashable contract holds regardless of which constructor a clone uses.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the actual code.

content.content:
1	"""Study definition — the adapter's research questions and pre-registration handle.
2	
3	A study definition binds an adapter's pieces together for one concrete run: which parser,
4	which mode, the optional claims source, any extra feature builders, and the research
5	questions. It lives in the instance repository (one repo per study, ADR-0002), not in the
6	engine. The engine reads it to drive the run and to anchor pre-registration.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	from dataclasses import dataclass
12	from enum import Enum
13	from typing import Sequence
14	
15	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ExternalClaimsSource
16	from assay_engine.contracts.features import FeatureBuilder
17	from assay_engine.contracts.parser import IngestionParser
18	
19	
20	class StudyMode(Enum):
21	    """The two modes from the charter. A study may combine both."""
22	
23	    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
24	    ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS = "adjudicate_external_claims"
25	
26	
27	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
28	class StudyDefinition:
29	    """Everything the engine needs to run one study, supplied by an adapter."""
30	
31	    name: str
32	    modes: frozenset[StudyMode]
33	    parser: IngestionParser
34	    research_questions: tuple[str, ...]
35	    feature_builders: tuple[FeatureBuilder, ...] = ()
36	    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource | None = None
37	
38	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
39	        if not self.name.strip():
40	            raise ValueError("StudyDefinition.name must be non-empty (it is the registry key)")
41	        if not self.modes:
42	            raise ValueError("StudyDefinition must declare at least one mode")
43	        needs_claims = StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS in self.modes
44	        if needs_claims and self.claims_source is None:
45	            raise ValueError("Adjudication mode requires a claims_source; none was provided")
46	        if not needs_claims and self.claims_source is not None:
47	            # A discovery-only study must not carry a claims source — it would risk
48	            # leaking external judgments into a pipeline that is meant to be blind.
49	            raise ValueError(
50	                "claims_source supplied but ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS mode not declared"
51	            )
52	
53	    @staticmethod
54	    def discovery(
55	        name: str,
56	        parser: IngestionParser,
57	        research_questions: Sequence[str],
58	        feature_builders: Sequence[FeatureBuilder] = (),
59	    ) -> "StudyDefinition":
60	        return StudyDefinition(
61	            name=name,
62	            modes=frozenset({StudyMode.DISCOVERY}),
63	            parser=parser,
64	            research_questions=tuple(research_questions),
65	            feature_builders=tuple(feature_builders),
66	        )
67

1	"""The two firewalls, enforced as runtime primitives (ADR-0005, METHODOLOGY.md §2).
2	
3	Both separations are enforced in code and exercised by tests — never asserted only in prose.
4	
5	Firewall A — claim-blindness:
6	    The baseline must be built blind to external claims. The *structural* guarantee is that
7	    the baseline builder is never handed an ``ExternalClaimsSource`` (its ``build`` signature
8	    takes only a ``Corpus``) and that ``StudyDefinition`` refuses a claims source outside
9	    adjudication mode. On top of that, :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` gives the claims source
10	    *custody*: hand the source to the guard, and the only way to obtain it is
11	    ``release()``/``access()``, which raise while the guard is sealed. Baseline construction
12	    runs inside ``sealed()``; adjudication runs after it.
13	
14	    Caveat (audit pass 1, issue #1): the guard cannot police a baseline builder that ignores
15	    it and reads external judgments smuggled into ``Corpus`` metadata/relations — that path
16	    is closed by the adapter contract (see schema.py), not by this guard. The guard is
17	    custodial defense-in-depth over the type-level separation, not the sole barrier.
18	
19	Firewall B — discover/confirm separation:
20	    The data used to discover a hypothesis must not be the data used to confirm it.
21	    :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit` holds disjoint, non-empty discovery and confirmation id
22	    sets and refuses to confirm on any id the discovery phase touched (or on an empty set).
23	"""
24	
25	from __future__ import annotations
26	
27	from contextlib import contextmanager
28	from dataclasses import dataclass
29	from typing import Generic, Iterable, Iterator, TypeVar
30	
31	C = TypeVar("C")
32	
33	
34	class FirewallViolation(RuntimeError):
35	    """Raised when a methodological firewall is breached at runtime."""
36	
37	
38	class ClaimBlindGuard(Generic[C]):
39	    """Firewall A. While sealed, the held claims source is unreachable.
40	
41	    Custodial usage (preferred) — the guard owns the source, so the baseline pipeline holds
42	    the guard rather than the source and cannot read claims while sealed::
43	
44	        guard = ClaimBlindGuard(claims_source)
45	        with guard.sealed():
46	            corpus = parser.parse(source)            # baseline build — claims unreachable
47	            baseline = build_baseline(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
48	        claims = guard.release()                      # adjudication — guard released
49	
50	    ``access(obj)`` remains for defensive checks at call sites that hold a reference directly:
51	    it returns ``obj`` when unsealed and raises while sealed.
52	    """
53	
54	    def __init__(self, claims_source: C | None = None) -> None:
55	        self._sealed = False
56	        self._claims_source = claims_source
57	
58	    @property
59	    def is_sealed(self) -> bool:
60	        return self._sealed
61	
62	    @contextmanager
63	    def sealed(self) -> Iterator["ClaimBlindGuard[C]"]:
64	        prior = self._sealed
65	        self._sealed = True
66	        try:
67	            yield self
68	        finally:
69	            self._sealed = prior
70	
71	    def release(self) -> C:
72	        """Return the custodial claims source, or raise while sealed / if none was given."""
73	        if self._sealed:
74	            raise FirewallViolation(
75	                "Firewall A: external claims released during blind baseline construction"
76	            )
77	        if self._claims_source is None:
78	            raise FirewallViolation("Firewall A: no claims source is under this guard's custody")
79	        return self._claims_source
80	
81	    def access(self, claims_source: C) -> C:
82	        """Return ``claims_source`` only if the guard is not sealed; else raise."""
83	        if self._sealed:
84	            raise FirewallViolation(
85	                "Firewall A: external claims accessed during blind baseline construction"
86	            )
87	        return claims_source
88	
89	
90	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
91	class DiscoverConfirmSplit:
92	    """Firewall B. Disjoint discovery / confirmation id partitions for unit-level claims."""
93	
94	    discovery_ids: frozenset[str]
95	    confirm_ids: frozenset[str]
96	
97	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
98	        overlap = self.discovery_ids & self.confirm_ids
99	        if overlap:
100	            raise FirewallViolation(
101	                f"Firewall B: discovery and confirmation partitions overlap on "
102	                f"{len(overlap)} id(s), e.g. {sorted(overlap)[:3]}"
103	            )
104	        # An empty confirmation partition means there is nothing held out to confirm on; a
105	        # unit-level held-out split with no held-out object is invalid by construction. The
106	        # genuine no-holdout case must use the whole-corpus null path (audit pass 1, issue #5).
107	        if not self.confirm_ids:
108	            raise FirewallViolation(
109	                "Firewall B: confirmation partition is empty — a unit-level held-out split "
110	                "must hold out at least one object (use whole-corpus confirmation otherwise)"
111	            )
112	
113	    @classmethod
114	    def from_partition(
115	        cls, discovery_ids: Iterable[str], confirm_ids: Iterable[str]
116	    ) -> "DiscoverConfirmSplit":
117	        return cls(frozenset(discovery_ids), frozenset(confirm_ids))
118	
119	    def assert_confirm_only(self, ids: Iterable[str]) -> None:
120	        """Raise if ``ids`` is empty or any of it was seen during discovery (double-dip)."""
121	        ids = frozenset(ids)
122	        if not ids:
123	            raise FirewallViolation(
124	                "Firewall B: confirmatory test evaluated zero held-out objects — a verdict "
125	                "cannot be produced from an empty confirmation set (audit pass 1, issue #5)"
126	            )
127	        leaked = ids & self.discovery_ids
128	        if leaked:
129	            raise FirewallViolation(
130	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test touched {len(leaked)} discovery id(s) — "
131	                f"double-dipping, e.g. {sorted(leaked)[:3]}"
132	            )
133	        unknown = ids - self.confirm_ids
134	        if unknown:
135	            raise FirewallViolation(
136	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test used {len(unknown)} id(s) outside the "
137	                f"confirmation partition, e.g. {sorted(unknown)[:3]}"
138	            )
139

content.text: The claim is confirmed by reading. Neither `__post_init__` coerces the set-typed fields to `frozenset`. Let me verify with a PoC and check how these records are actually used (whether they're hashed/used as registry keys, which determines real-world severity).

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c '
from assay_engine.contracts.study import StudyDefinition, StudyMode
from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit

class P:
    def parse(self, *a, **k): ...

d = StudyDefinition(name="s", modes={StudyMode.DISCOVERY}, parser=P(), research_questions=("q",))
print("modes type:", type(d.modes).__name__)
try:
    hash(d)
    print("hash OK")
except TypeError as e:
    print("hash FAILED:", e)

# in-place mutation possible?
try:
    d.modes.add(StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS)
    print("mutated modes:", d.modes)
except Exception as e:
    print("mutate raised:", e)

s = DiscoverConfirmSplit(discovery_ids={"a"}, confirm_ids={"b"})
print("dcs discovery type:", type(s.discovery_ids).__name__)
try:
    hash(s); print("dcs hash OK")
except TypeError as e:
    print("dcs hash FAILED:", e)
'
input.description: PoC mutable set in frozen dataclasses

content.content:
modes type: set
hash FAILED: unhashable type: 'set'
mutated modes: {<StudyMode.DISCOVERY: 'discovery'>, <StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS: 'adjudicate_external_claims'>}
dcs discovery type: set
dcs hash FAILED: unhashable type: 'set'

content.text: PoC confirms both claims. Now let me check whether these records are actually hashed/used as dict keys or in sets anywhere in the engine — that determines whether this is a real production defect vs theoretical.

content.text: Let me check whether the engine actually relies on hashing/immutability of these records (e.g., dict keys, set membership, provenance/pre-registration hashing).

content.content:

content.text: There's a `_frozen.py` and `_canonical.py` that handle freezing. Let me check `_canonical.py` since it's used for pre-registration locking — and check whether StudyDefinition flows through the pre-registration / canonical path (which is the "pre-registration anchoring" claim).

content.content:
1	"""Type-faithful canonical hashing — the engine's single content-digest primitive.
2	
3	Two unrelated places need a *stable, type-faithful* content hash: the baseline determinism
4	harness (so the same inputs reproduce the same baseline) and pre-registration (so a lock
5	binds the exact hypothesis content). Both must distinguish values that merely share a
6	``str()`` — ``int 1`` from ``str "1"``, ``date(2020,1,1)`` from ``"2020-01-01"`` — or the
7	hash is a liability rather than a guarantee.
8	
9	This module is the one implementation, hoisted here so it sits *below* both
10	``methodology`` and ``baseline`` in the import graph (neither may depend on the other). It is
11	dependency-free and pure. Anything it cannot canonicalize reproducibly is refused **loudly**:
12	a default (address-based) ``repr`` would make the digest differ every process and silently
13	break the guarantee it exists to provide.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import datetime as _dt
19	import decimal
20	import hashlib
21	import json
22	import uuid
23	from typing import Any, Mapping
24	
25	
26	def keytag(key: Any) -> str:
27	    """A type-faithful, sortable string for a mapping key, so distinct keys that happen to
28	    share a ``str()`` (e.g. int ``1`` vs str ``"1"``) do NOT collide."""
29	    return f"{type(key).__name__}::{key!r}"
30	
31	
32	def canonical_plain(value: Any) -> Any:
33	    """Canonicalize ``value`` into a fully JSON-native, type-faithful structure for hashing.
34	
35	    Mappings become a sorted list of ``[typed-key, value]`` pairs; bytes, sets, and any
36	    non-JSON-native leaf are tagged with their type rather than stringified. Nothing is coerced
37	    with ``str()``, so the content hash distinguishes values that share a ``str()``.
38	    """
39	    if value is None or isinstance(value, (str, bool, int, float)):
40	        return value  # JSON-native scalars; json encodes 1, "1", true, 1.0 distinctly
41	    if isinstance(value, bytes):
42	        return {"__bytes__": value.hex()}
43	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
44	        pairs = sorted(
45	            ([keytag(k), canonical_plain(v)] for k, v in value.items()), key=lambda p: p[0]
46	        )
47	        return {"__map__": pairs}
48	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
49	        return [canonical_plain(v) for v in value]
50	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
51	        return {"__set__": sorted((canonical_plain(v) for v in value), key=repr)}
52	    # A small allowlist of leaf types with a value-faithful canonical form.
53	    if isinstance(value, (_dt.date, _dt.datetime, _dt.time)):
54	        return {"__temporal__": value.isoformat()}
55	    if isinstance(value, decimal.Decimal):
56	        return {"__decimal__": str(value)}
57	    if isinstance(value, uuid.UUID):
58	        return {"__uuid__": str(value)}
59	    # Anything else is refused LOUDLY rather than repr-tagged: a default (address-based) repr
60	    # would make the digest differ every process, silently breaking the guarantee.
61	    raise TypeError(
62	        f"cannot canonicalize a leaf of type "
63	        f"{type(value).__module__}.{type(value).__qualname__!r} reproducibly — convert it to a "
64	        "JSON-native value, bytes, date/datetime/Decimal/UUID before hashing"
65	    )
66	
67	
68	def canonical_json(value: Any) -> str:
69	    """The canonical JSON text of ``value`` — stable across processes and key orderings."""
70	    return json.dumps(canonical_plain(value), sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False)
71	
72	
73	def hash_bytes(data: bytes) -> str:
74	    return hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
75	
76	
77	def hash_text(text: str) -> str:
78	    return hash_bytes(text.encode("utf-8"))
79	
80	
81	def hash_value(value: Any) -> str:
82	    """Stable, type-faithful content hash of an arbitrary value.
83	
84	    ``canonical_plain`` canonicalizes everything to JSON-native form first, so ``json.dumps``
85	    needs no ``default=`` coercion — anything it still cannot encode is a real bug to surface,
86	    not to silently stringify.
87	    """
88	    return hash_text(canonical_json(value))
89

content.text:
Note `canonical_plain` handles both `set` and `frozenset` identically (line 50) — so the canonical/pre-registration hash path does NOT depend on `modes` being a frozenset; a plain set hashes the same. So the actual pre-registration anchoring is unaffected by this issue.

Now the key question: is `StudyDefinition` itself ever hashed (Python `hash()`) or put in a set/dict key anywhere in the engine? The grep showed it's used in `pipeline.py` as a field, and `registry.py` stores factories by name (string keys). Let me confirm StudyDefinition/DiscoverConfirmSplit are never directly Python-hashed.

src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:113:        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
tests/test_baseline.py:48:    assert hash_value({"a": 1, "b": 2}) == hash_value({"b": 2, "a": 1})

content.text:
Confirmed: `StudyDefinition` and `DiscoverConfirmSplit` are never Python-`hash()`-ed nor used as set/dict keys in the engine. The registry keys on `name` (string). The pre-registration/canonical hash path treats `set` and `frozenset` identically. So the immutability/hashability contract is declared (`frozen=True, slots=True`) but not actually depended upon at runtime.

Let me check how the engine compares the `_frozen.py` philosophy — there's an explicit `freeze` helper and module philosophy that frozen records must really be frozen. This establishes that the codebase has a stated invariant that the assessor's finding violates.

content.content:
1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
62	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
63	
64	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
65	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
66	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
67	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
68	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
69	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
70	    """
71	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
72	        return value
73	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
74	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
75	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
76	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
77	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
78	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
79	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
80	        return bytes(value)
81	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
82	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
83	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
84	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
85	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
86	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
87	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
88	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
89	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
90	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
91	        )
92	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
93	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
94	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
95	    try:
96	        hash(value)
97	    except TypeError as exc:
98	        raise TypeError(
99	            f"freeze: leaf of type {type(value).__name__!r} is not hashable and has no "
100	            "known immutable conversion; a frozen value record cannot contain it"
101	        ) from exc
102	    return value
103	
104	
105	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
106	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`."""
107	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
108

content.text:
This is decisive. `_frozen.py`'s entire module docstring (audit pass 1, issue #7; pass 2, issue #19; pass 3, issues #26/#27) documents exactly this class of bug as a stated invariant the codebase commits to: a `frozen=True` dataclass that *looks* hashable but raises `TypeError` when hashed, and that is mutable-in-place, is treated as a genuine defect to be fixed across the engine. Other dataclasses normalize their collection fields through `freeze` in `__post_init__`.

`StudyDefinition.modes` and `DiscoverConfirmSplit.discovery_ids/confirm_ids` are the two `frozen=True` collection-typed fields that were *not* given this normalization on the raw constructor path. This is a real regression/gap relative to the codebase's own stated invariant — the assessor's finding is internally consistent with the project's hardening standard, not merely a style nit.

Now let me calibrate severity honestly. The runtime engine never Python-hashes these records, and the canonical/pre-registration hash path treats set and frozenset identically — so there is no current functional breakage in production flows. The defect is: (a) a latent landmine (any future `hash()`/set-membership/dedup on these records breaks), and (b) the documented frozen+immutable+hashable contract is false for the public raw constructor, which a clone author can legitimately use (test_contract.py:59,70 and test_pipeline.py:521 already construct StudyDefinition directly). Let me confirm those direct-construction tests don't hash.

content.text:
Existing tests pass frozenset explicitly. So no current test fails, and no engine code path breaks today. The defect is a genuine but latent contract violation.

I have enough to render judgment.

Assessment:
- The finding is technically accurate and PoC-confirmed: both `StudyDefinition.__post_init__` and `DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__` fail to coerce their `frozenset`-annotated fields, so a plain `set` is silently accepted, making a `frozen=True, slots=True` record neither hashable (`hash()` raises `TypeError`) nor immutable (`.modes.add(...)` mutates in place).
- It is internally consistent with the codebase's *own* explicitly stated invariant: `_frozen.py` documents this exact bug class (frozen record that looks hashable but isn't, and is mutable in place) as a defect that was hunted and fixed across the engine in pass 1 (#7), pass 2 (#19), pass 3 (#26/#27). The `modes` and `discovery_ids/confirm_ids` collection fields are the remaining `frozen` collection fields that escaped that normalization on the raw-constructor path. So this is a real gap pass-1 missed, not a re-report of #101-#122 and not a style preference.
- However, no current production code path Python-hashes these records or uses them as set/dict keys (registry keys on `name`; pipeline holds them as plain fields), and the pre-registration/canonical hash path (`_canonical.py`) treats `set` and `frozenset` identically — so there is no live functional breakage today. It is a latent landmine plus a false public-API contract.

Severity: MEDIUM is slightly high for something with zero current functional impact; but it is a real public-API contract violation on a documented frozen/hashable value object that a clone author can hit via the supported raw constructor, and it directly contradicts a codebase invariant the team has been hardening to. LOW-to-MEDIUM. I'll set MEDIUM, matching the assessor, because it is a genuine production-readiness gap in the public contract layer that the project's own standard treats as a fixable defect, and the suggested fix is correct.

input.reasoning:
CONFIRMED, real production-readiness defect. Both StudyDefinition.__post_init__ (study.py:38-51) and DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__ (firewalls.py:97-111) declare frozenset-typed fields on @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) records but never coerce/validate them. The raw constructors — which are the public API surface a clone uses (and which existing tests test_contract.py:59,70 and test_pipeline.py:521 already exercise directly) — silently accept a plain set. PoC verified: StudyDefinition(..., modes={StudyMode.DISCOVERY}).modes is a set; hash(definition) raises TypeError: unhashable type 'set'; and definition.modes.add(...) mutates a 'frozen' record in place. Same for DiscoverConfirmSplit with a plain set.

This is internally consistent with — and a gap in — the codebase's OWN stated invariant. src/assay_engine/_frozen.py exists precisely to fix this exact bug class for frozen records: its docstring (citing audit pass 1 issue #7, pass 2 #19, pass 3 #26/#27) calls out that frozen=True auto-generates __hash__ over all fields so instances 'look hashable but raise TypeError the moment they are hashed,' and that frozen only blocks rebinding while the contained collection stays mutable in place. Every other affected dataclass normalizes its collection fields through freeze() in __post_init__. modes / discovery_ids / confirm_ids are the remaining frozen collection fields that escaped that normalization on the raw-constructor path. So this is a genuine miss, not a re-report of pass-1 #101-#122 nor a style nit, and aligns with the project's hardening standard.

Calibration: I keep MEDIUM (assessor's rating) but note it is at the LOW/MEDIUM boundary because there is no LIVE functional breakage today: I confirmed no engine code path Python-hashes these records or uses them as set/dict keys (registry.py keys on the name string; pipeline.py holds them as plain fields), and the pre-registration/canonical hash path (_canonical.py canonical_plain lines 50-51) treats set and frozenset identically, so pre-registration anchoring is unaffected by the wrong runtime type. The defect is therefore (a) a latent landmine that breaks the instant any future code hashes/dedups/set-keys a definition or split, and (b) a false public-API contract: a documented frozen+immutable+hashable value object that is in fact neither when built via its supported raw constructor. The suggested fix (object.__setattr__ frozenset coercion, ideally with element-type validation, in both __post_init__ methods) is correct and matches the pattern used elsewhere in the engine.
input.evidence:
PoC (run with /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python): StudyDefinition(name='s', modes={StudyMode.DISCOVERY}, parser=P(), research_questions=('q',)) -> modes type: set; hash FAILED: unhashable type: 'set'; d.modes.add(...) succeeded mutating the 'frozen' record to {DISCOVERY, ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS}. DiscoverConfirmSplit(discovery_ids={'a'}, confirm_ids={'b'}) -> discovery_ids type: set; hash FAILED: unhashable type: 'set'.

Code: study.py:27-66 — @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True); modes: frozenset[StudyMode]; __post_init__ checks emptiness/claims but never frozenset()-coerces self.modes; only the discovery() factory (line 62) coerces. firewalls.py:90-117 — same shape; __post_init__ validates overlap/emptiness but never coerces; only from_partition() (line 117) coerces.

Codebase invariant this violates: _frozen.py:1-16 docstring states frozen records with collection fields 'look hashable but raise TypeError the moment they are hashed' and 'the contained dict stays mutable in place' as a defect fixed in pass 1 (#7), pass 2 (#19), pass 3 (#26/#27); other dataclasses normalize via freeze() in __post_init__.

No live impact found: grep shows StudyDefinition/DiscoverConfirmSplit never Python-hashed nor used as set/dict keys (registry.py keys on name string; pipeline.py:126,130 hold them as plain fields); _canonical.py:50-51 hashes set and frozenset identically so pre-registration is unaffected by the wrong type. Existing tests (test_contract.py:61,72; test_pipeline.py) pass frozenset explicitly, so no current test fails.

content.text:
I've completed my independent verification and submitted the structured output.

Verdict: TRUE positive, MEDIUM severity.

The finding is PoC-confirmed in both records. `StudyDefinition.__post_init__` (study.py:38-51) and `DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__` (firewalls.py:97-111) never coerce their `frozenset`-typed fields, so the raw constructors silently accept a plain `set`, producing a `frozen=True` record that raises `TypeError` when hashed and is mutable in place.

Key supporting evidence beyond the assessor's report:
- This exact bug class is the codebase's own documented invariant: `src/assay_engine/_frozen.py` was created to fix "frozen records that look hashable but raise TypeError when hashed, and stay mutable in place," across pass 1 (#7), pass 2 (#19), pass 3 (#26/#27). `modes`/`discovery_ids`/`confirm_ids` are the frozen collection fields that escaped that normalization. So this is a genuine gap, not a re-report of #101-#122 and not style.
- I downgraded the practical urgency context (but kept MEDIUM): no live engine path Python-hashes these records or uses them as set/dict keys (registry keys on the `name` string; pipeline holds them as plain fields), and `_canonical.py` hashes `set` and `frozenset` identically, so pre-registration anchoring is not actually corrupted today. The defect is a latent landmine plus a false public-API contract that a clone author can hit via the supported raw constructor.

The suggested fix is correct: add `object.__setattr__(self, "modes", frozenset(self.modes))` (ideally with element-type validation) to both `__post_init__` methods.

Relevant files:
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/contracts/study.py
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/firewalls.py
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (documents the invariant)
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_canonical.py (shows pre-registration path is unaffected)
